cross_system.title: Cross-system signal: credit_card flow leading stock — origination headroom
cross_system.body: Credit card spending is slowing down, which usually happens before credit card loans start shrinking. When people spend less on their cards, they typically pay down their balances over the next few months, causing total credit card debt to drop. This gives banks a few months' head start to see what's coming.
cross_system.implication: Banks should prepare for lower credit card loan growth and adjust their lending plans before the slowdown hits their loan books.
chain: People are spending less money on their credit cards
Lower spending usually leads to people paying down their card balances over the next few months
Banks can spot this early and adjust their credit card lending before loan growth actually slows

cross_system.title: Unsecured retail credit appetite — expanding (5/5 measurements observed)
cross_system.body: Personal loans for credit cards and other purposes grew 3.78 percent and 12.7 percent year-on-year, while credit card balances outstanding rose 8.15 percent. Consumer durable loans fell 3.38 percent. Most unsecured lending is expanding, meaning banks are willing to lend more to people without collateral because they see demand and acceptable risk.
cross_system.implication: A bank should grow its unsecured personal loan book now, especially in the other personal loan category growing at 12.7 percent, while watching credit quality closely since these loans have no asset backing.
chain: Banks are lending more personal loans without collateral, with growth of 3.78 percent and 12.7 percent in two categories
People are borrowing more on credit cards too, with outstanding balances up 8.15 percent, showing steady demand for unsecured credit
This is a good time to lend in these categories, but the bank must monitor repayment carefully because there is no property or gold to recover if borrowers default

basis.headline: Unsecured retail credit appetite → expanding
chain: Measured inputs: Credit Card Outstanding +1.3% YoY, Other Personal Loans +12.5% YoY, Consumer Durables -2.6% YoY, Credit card spend rising, Credit cards outstanding +8.5% YoY.
Member directions combine — measures and proxies count as-is, contra-indicators flipped — giving an expanding read on 5 of 5 measurements.

The willingness of households to carry — and lenders to extend — unsecured retail credit. No pipeline measures it directly: SIBC measures the unsecured stock (credit-card outstanding, other personal loans, consumer durables), payments measures the flow (credit-card spend) and reach (cards in force). Together they bound the construct: when stock, flow and reach move together the appetite read is unambiguous; when they diverge, the divergence itself is the signal.
Direction sustained across two composed periods → status active.

cross_system.title: Card-based consumption activity — expanding (5/5 measurements observed)
cross_system.body: Card terminals grew 1.45 percent, vehicle loans jumped 17.97 percent, but consumer durable loans fell 3.38 percent. People are borrowing heavily to buy cars and trucks, using cards a bit more, yet pulling back on financing fridges and TVs. The pattern says households are spending selectively—big-ticket transport yes, home goods no.
cross_system.implication: A bank should push auto finance hard, keep card offers steady, and avoid chasing consumer-durable portfolios until that 3.38 percent decline reverses.
chain: Card infrastructure is growing at 1.45 percent and vehicle lending is surging at 17.97 percent, so people are transacting more and financing expensive transport.
At the same time consumer durable lending dropped 3.38 percent, meaning households are skipping loans for appliances and electronics.
Banks should allocate capital to auto loans where demand is strong and wait on durable-goods credit until appetite returns.

basis.headline: Card-based consumption activity → expanding
chain: Measured inputs: Credit card spend rising, Debit card spend falling, POS terminals -0.5% YoY, Consumer Durables -2.6% YoY, Vehicle Loans +17.3% YoY.
Member directions combine — measures and proxies count as-is, contra-indicators flipped — giving an expanding read on 5 of 5 measurements.

Household consumption as expressed through card rails. Payments measures the transaction flows (credit + debit card spend) directly; acceptance infrastructure (POS terminals) proxies the merchant side of the same activity; SIBC's consumer-durable and vehicle loan stocks proxy the credit-financed share of it.
Direction sustained across two composed periods → status active.

cross_system.title: Unsecured spend–stock cycle — running (3/3 segments live)
cross_system.body: This is a feedback cycle read across both pipelines: spending, balances, and appetite feed each other. The basis below shows each segment and whether it is currently running with the cycle or against it.
cross_system.implication: A fully-running cycle compounds; a segment moving against it is the early warning that the cycle is turning. Watch the reversed segment.
chain: Segment states: Unsecured retail credit appetite → Credit card spend (value) — running; Credit card spend (value) → Credit Card Outstanding — running; Credit Card Outstanding → Unsecured retail credit appetite — running.
A loop fires only when every segment runs in its expected direction; 3 of 3 are live, so the cycle is running.

Appetite expresses as card spend (flow), spend accumulates into card balances (stock), and growing balances evidence the appetite that started the cycle. The loop fires only when all three segments move the same way — a clean composed read that the unsecured retail cycle is running (or, reversed, unwinding). Segments span both pipelines plus the confirmed flow→stock cross-edge.

basis.headline: Unsecured spend–stock cycle → active reinforcing

members.label: Unsecured retail credit appetite → Credit card spend (value)
Credit card spend (value) → Credit Card Outstanding
Credit Card Outstanding → Unsecured retail credit appetite

chain: Segment states: Unsecured retail credit appetite → Credit card spend (value) — running; Credit card spend (value) → Credit Card Outstanding — running; Credit Card Outstanding → Unsecured retail credit appetite — running.
A loop fires only when every segment runs in its expected direction; 3 of 3 are live, so the cycle is running.

Appetite expresses as card spend (flow), spend accumulates into card balances (stock), and growing balances evidence the appetite that started the cycle. The loop fires only when all three segments move the same way — a clean composed read that the unsecured retail cycle is running (or, reversed, unwinding). Segments span both pipelines plus the confirmed flow→stock cross-edge.

sibc.title: Co-lending & warehouse-financing infrastructure
sibc.body: Small business credit is growing at 26.18 percent this year and now makes up 23.06 percent of all business lending. Growth in micro and small business lending jumped by 23.77 percentage points, driven by government systems like UDYAM registration and GST data that make it easier to check if tiny businesses are real and creditworthy. Banks cannot reach thousands of small borrowers alone, so they need partners who can do the ground work of finding customers and verifying them.
sibc.implication: Banks should build partnerships with fintech companies and NBFCs who specialize in finding and checking small borrowers, using co-lending or warehouse financing to fund these loans while the partners handle customer acquisition and verification.
chain: Government systems like UDYAM and GST now create digital records that prove small businesses exist and show their real sales, making them easier to lend to
Small business lending is growing at 26.18 percent because banks can now trust the data, but banks lack the staff and local presence to find and serve thousands of tiny borrowers directly

Banks need local partners who know the customers and can verify them on the ground, then fund those loans through co-lending or warehouse lines while the partner does the hard work of reaching borrowers
sibc._driver: MSME formalisation via UDYAM registration + GST data

sibc.title: Gold loan market entry / deepening for banks
sibc.body: Banks are growing gold loans by 105.5 percent compared to last year, and this growth has been climbing for 12.0 periods in a row. Gold prices jumped sharply between 2023 and 2026, so customers can now borrow much more money using the same gold jewelry or coins as security. Banks are winning customers from finance companies because they have local branches where staff can check the gold is real and store it safely, plus they charge lower interest rates.
sibc.implication: The bank should open more gold loan counters in branches, train staff to test gold quickly, and advertise lower rates than finance companies to pull in customers who want bigger loans against their existing gold.
chain: Gold prices rose a lot from 2023 to 2026, so the same necklace or coin is now worth more money.
Because the gold is worth more, banks can lend bigger amounts while still staying safe, and customers want those bigger loans.
Banks with local branches can check and store the gold easily, so they are taking customers away from finance companies and growing fast for 12.0 periods straight.

sibc.title: MSME origination infrastructure investment window
sibc.body: Small business lending is growing at 32.68 pct per year, and these tiny businesses now make up 23.06 pct of all MSME lending. Their growth rate jumped by 23.77 pp recently, while bigger MSME borrowers are growing much slower—the gap between small and large is 11.8 pp. Banks can now check if these small businesses can repay loans using government registration systems and GST records, which makes lending to them safer and faster.
sibc.implication: A bank should invest in systems that connect to UDYAM registration and GST data so it can quickly approve loans for small businesses that were previously too hard to assess.
chain: Government created digital records (UDYAM registration and GST filings) that show whether a tiny business is real and earning money
Banks can now check these records instantly instead of guessing, so they feel safe lending to small borrowers they ignored before
Small business lending accelerated by 23.77 pp because banks can finally verify creditworthiness at scale
sibc._driver: MSME formalisation via UDYAM registration + GST data

sibc.title: PLI anchor-supplier chain finance
sibc.body: The government is paying big manufacturers to build more factories, and those manufacturers need more parts from smaller suppliers. Lending to suppliers in basic metals grew 22.61 pct and 18.43 pct, chemicals grew 22.63 pct and 21.8 pct and 14.2 pct and 7.43 pct, and engineering grew 23.85 pct and 38.27 pct. These suppliers are safer to lend to because they have contracts with large established companies that guarantee payment.
sibc.implication: The bank should lend more to small and medium suppliers in metals, chemicals, and engineering who have confirmed orders from big manufacturers expanding under the government scheme.
chain: Government pays big manufacturers to expand factories under PLI scheme
Those manufacturers need more parts so they order from smaller suppliers in metals, chemicals, and engineering
Suppliers with confirmed orders from big companies are safer to lend to because payment is guaranteed

sibc.title: PSLC market depth from PSL reclassification surplus
sibc.body: Housing loans that count toward priority sector lending jumped 38.98% year-over-year after RBI raised the ceiling in October 2024. Banks did not actually lend more money; the rule change simply let them reclassify existing housing loans as priority sector loans. This created instant surplus PSL credits that banks can now sell to other banks who are short on their required priority sector targets.
sibc.implication: A bank should buy PSL credits from banks with surplus or sell its own surplus credits if it has reclassified housing loans, because the market now has more supply and prices may be better for both buyers and sellers.
chain: RBI raised the housing loan ceiling that counts toward priority sector lending in October 2024
Banks reclassified existing housing loans under the new ceiling without making new loans, creating a 38.98% jump in reported priority sector housing loans
Banks with surplus credits can now sell them to banks that need to meet their priority sector targets, making the PSL credit market deeper and more liquid
sibc._driver: RBI PSL housing loan ceiling revision (Oct 2024)

sibc.title: Renewable energy project finance — next capex wave
sibc.body: Old infrastructure lending is shrinking — some sectors fell -44.06% and -26.03% this year — while renewable energy sectors jumped 74.02% and 21.47%. Banks already have teams that know how to finance big, long-term projects like highways and power plants, and those same skills work for solar, wind, and green hydrogen projects. Government renewable energy targets mean these new projects now need the same kind of financing that old infrastructure used to get.
sibc.implication: Banks should move their project finance teams from old infrastructure sectors into renewable energy lending, using the same credit skills they already have.
chain: Old infrastructure projects are finishing and paying back loans, so lending in those sectors is falling fast
Government is pushing hard on renewable energy targets, creating demand for big new solar, wind, and hydrogen projects
Banks can use their existing project finance skills to lend for these new renewable projects instead of waiting for old infrastructure to come back
sibc._via: Project lifecycle phase → new origination vs repayment

sibc.title: Services sector credit entry / scale-up
sibc.body: Services sector lending is growing at 20.41% per year and now makes up 28.28% of all bank credit. Banks are lending more through NBFCs that serve services companies — this channel grew 19.71% and is strengthening. Three services sub-sectors are growing extremely fast: one at 54.95%, another at 40.97%, and a third at 33.69% per year.
sibc.implication: The bank should increase lending to NBFCs that specialize in services companies, especially those serving the three fastest-growing sub-sectors, because this is where credit demand is surging and the NBFC channel is recovering strongly.
chain: Services companies need more credit and are growing fast, so they are borrowing more from banks and NBFCs
Banks are lending more through NBFCs because rules around bank-to-NBFC lending have become easier after a difficult period
Three specific services sub-sectors are growing much faster than the rest, so NBFCs serving those areas will need the most funding
sibc._driver: NBFC credit channel recovery post risk-weight cycle

sibc.title: EV-specific vehicle credit products
sibc.body: RETIRED at first FOUNDATION (2026-06-10). No external source verified for EV share of vehicle loans or EV-specific credit risk differentiation. No L1 signal separating EV from ICE vehicle loans. Returns only when EV share of vehicle loans is measurable in L1 (e.g., VAHAN registration data ingestion).
sibc.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.

sibc.title: Infrastructure repayment wave with insufficient new origination pipeline
sibc.body: Banks over-indexed to traditional infrastructure credit face a structural squeeze: the FY22-24 project book moves to repayment (compressing balances) while the next-cycle pipeline (renewables, data centres) is slower to ramp. Banks without next-cycle project finance capability face credit portfolio contraction.
sibc.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: As a project cohort moves from construction (drawdown) to operations (repayment), net credit balance declines unless new origination offsets it — suppressing growth even while existing books remain large.
This is being driven by Infrastructure supercycle completing (FY22–24).
sibc._via: Project lifecycle phase → new origination vs repayment

sibc.title: CIC reporting compliance gaps creating off-balance-sheet credit risk
sibc.body: RBI Digital Lending Guidelines (2022/2025) require BNPL and digital credit to report to Credit Information Companies. However, compliance may be incomplete among newer digital lenders operating under first-loss guarantee structures or co-lending arrangements where the regulated entity's on-book exposure is partially shielded. If reporting gaps exist, the true unsecured credit stress is understated in bank balance sheets and in SIBC data.
sibc.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: Higher risk weights raise the regulatory capital cost per unit of exposure, compressing origination economics for unsecured retail products until reversed.
This is being driven by RBI risk-weight hike on unsecured credit (Nov 2023).
sibc._driver: RBI risk-weight hike on unsecured credit (Nov 2023)
sibc._via: Regulatory capital weight → unsecured origination economics

atm_pos.title: Credit card penetration deepening — PSB share gap is the opening
atm_pos.body: India has 120454115.0 credit cards outstanding, growing at 8.47 percent year-on-year. Private banks hold 70.91 percent of these cards, while public sector banks hold only 23.83 percent — even though public banks control about 64 percent of the debit card base. This gap means public banks have millions of existing customers with debit cards who could be upgraded to credit cards at much lower cost than finding brand-new customers.
atm_pos.implication: Public sector banks should launch targeted upgrade programs offering credit cards to their existing debit card holders, capturing share in a growing market where they are currently underrepresented.
chain: Public banks have far more debit card customers than their credit card share suggests
Upgrading an existing customer costs less than acquiring a stranger
By offering credit cards to people who already bank with them, public banks can grow faster and cheaper

atm_pos.title: Rural cash distribution via Micro ATM / Business Correspondent networks
atm_pos.body: India has 1358241.0 micro ATMs, but the number fell -8.11 percent recently. These small cash machines work in villages and towns where a full bank branch or big ATM costs too much to run. Even though the count dropped, people in these areas still need cash every day, and micro ATMs cost far less to set up than a real branch.
atm_pos.implication: A bank should keep or grow its network of micro ATMs and business correspondents in rural areas because customers there depend on cash and this channel costs much less than building branches.
chain: Micro ATM numbers dropped -8.11 percent to 1358241.0 units, showing some operators pulled back
Rural customers still need daily cash but full branches are too expensive to justify in small towns
Banks that maintain or expand low-cost micro ATM networks can serve this demand profitably where others cannot

atm_pos.title: Embedded transit and tap-to-pay payment products
atm_pos.body: Credit card spending in the 'other' category jumped 2365.96 percent in value and 714.01 percent in volume year-on-year. This means people are using cards in completely new places — likely transit gates, vending machines, or small merchants that just got tap-to-pay terminals. The growth is so extreme it shows a new habit forming, not just existing users spending more.
atm_pos.implication: Banks should rush to issue cards that work on these new payment rails (transit tap or UPI-linked credit) and sign up the merchants or transit systems rolling out the terminals, because whoever gets in first will own the new spending habit.
chain: New terminals (transit gates, small shops) suddenly accept card taps, creating places people never used cards before
Consumers try it once, find it faster than cash or apps, and keep using it — so volume and value explode
The bank that issues the card or runs the terminal network captures all that new spending and builds the habit before competitors arrive
atm_pos._driver: Embedded card payment infrastructure rollout (transit NFC or RuPay-credit-on-UPI)
atm_pos._via: Embedded payment rails → new card-spend surface

atm_pos.title: Credit card market concentrated in top-5 private banks
atm_pos.body: The top-5 private banks control approximately 71% of credit card outstanding. The largest single issuer holds ~20% of the market. This concentration creates systemic fragility — as demonstrated when a single payment bank's exit caused a market-wide debit card step-down. Similar disruption from a major credit card issuer would have proportionally larger effects.
atm_pos.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.

atm_pos.title: Debit card base structural decline reducing cash access for underbanked
atm_pos.body: The debit card base is contracting through payment bank exits and lifecycle purges. This reduces the potential transaction base for populations that depend on debit cards for cash access and digital payments — particularly lower-income and rural segments without credit cards or reliable UPI-linked accounts.
atm_pos.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: Regulatory wind-down of a major issuer forces cancellation of its entire card portfolio, producing a single-period step-down in the stock concentrated in that issuer's lender category.
This is being driven by RBI enforcement action against Paytm Payments Bank (Jan–Apr 2026).
atm_pos._driver: RBI enforcement action against Paytm Payments Bank (Jan–Apr 2026)

atm_pos.title: Payment acceptance bifurcation: POS contracting while UPI grows
atm_pos.body: POS terminal network contraction while UPI QR expands creates an acceptance gap: merchants who drop POS terminals cannot accept international cards, EMI transactions, premium credit card features, or POS-authenticated payments. Customers without UPI-linked accounts face reduced options at merchants who have switched fully to UPI.
atm_pos.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: Zero MDR on UPI makes QR acceptance structurally cheaper than card POS (no hardware, no per-txn fee), driving QR deployment while suppressing POS-terminal investment. Polarity is per-target: + for QR, − for POS.
This is being driven by Zero MDR policy on UPI transactions (NPCI/GoI).
atm_pos._driver: Zero MDR policy on UPI transactions (NPCI/GoI)
